speaker: interviewer
text: Interview with the Leopold Zylberman This is tape four. We'll take you-- you had just landed on the other side of the River Bug.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, so we landed on the beach bank. And the smugglers sailed or rode away very quickly.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were they Jewish?

speaker: interviewee
text: I wouldn't have a clue. But the other passengers were Jewish. And he told-- we were told that down to the right is a township called Drohitchin, and past Drohitchin there is a township called [NON-ENGLISH]. And there you'll find the railway, and everything is all right. Good luck. And we started walking. And there was a Jewish couple with their baby.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was their name? Do you know?

speaker: interviewee
text: I wouldn't know. I never saw them again. And we asked them, for goodness bloody sake, you see, you-- you make this stop crying. Because we all get arrested. And this woman went out with a big mouth, you see, what do you mean? Now we are in our country. In country for laborers, and you so and so, and et cetera. You still speak Polish. And we won't speak with you. Anyway, you see just out of nowhere, you see the red army just came to us with bayonets, and hands up. And we knew that-- and of course we had no options. So they divided us into groups, and every two or three you see our group was run by a soldier. We walked and walked, and came up to a sort of a place, which was an old house converted as a jail or something. They put us into the bunker. They were escorting around. But we were sitting. And we didn't know what to do.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was Lola with you still?

speaker: interviewee
text: With Lola, with Romek and his wife, and another guy. And in the morning, you see we have canteens with water or something. And we just helped ourselves. And in the morning, you see, the officer came and with two soldiers. And they took a group of people. And they gave us marching orders.

speaker: interviewee
text: And you see, one was following the other, and Rubin got hysterics. Anyway, just out of the blue comes up a friend of mine, a school mate. He said, what are you doing here? I say, what are you doing here? Then-- and he said, I have an uncle. And he said, what do they do to you? They want to take us back to the German side. He said, we don't need you. That means we said that we wanted to be with the Russians, with workers, et cetera. But if you come in this, you stay where you are, and you do your own revolution.

speaker: interviewee
text: Because you see, we understood a little bit of Russian, you see, since childhood, you know. And do your own revolution. So he said, look. When you're on the boat, leave a few bucks for the fisherman. And he will take you back, you see. And so we did. We went on the boat. And he was sort of going to the other coast, the other bank of the river, and just sailing along side. And then he came back. And he said, that I couldn't let them out. Because the German was shooting. And I don't to risk my life. And the officer, you say that you're not welcome here. We don't want you. You must make your way back to your side, to your town, to your people. Because if we meet you again, we'll shoot you.

speaker: interviewer
text: The Russian officer said this?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. And so we already knew. That means it was a sort of a routine, because they couldn't send us back. And we went back. This friend of mine's uncle, lived in Drohitchin. And he had an inn. No. Actually, they took us back to the jail. You see, and this uncle of my friend had the inn and he and those Russians used to come to drink with him.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the name of your friend?

speaker: interviewee
text: Semiatycki, Julek Semiatycki.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you spell that please?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. First name, Julek, J-U-L-E-K. The second name was S-E-M-I-A-T-Y-C-K-I.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was he Jewish?

speaker: interviewee
text: Jewish, he was unfortunately.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why do you say that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Because he's not alive anymore. You see, he went back to Poland to pick up his wife, never came back. And-- and a few hours, an hour or so later, the officer came and sent us. You go back to where you are or we'll shoot you. You know that just mix up the time.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we went to the inn. We had a good meal. And then the next day we walked to a township called [NON-ENGLISH], and got into a train. Because the trains were going without attention, you see, packed with people. Could I just clarify something here? You were told by the Russian officer to go back. But they didn't escort you?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. That was the idea which the uncle arranged, you know. So he just let us free. But he wants us out of sight.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you got on this train now?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. And we went to Bialystok. And Bialystok was flooded with refugees. We had some friends there, and some were there. Some were not. We had customers. And we were accommodated in a hotel.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the name of the hotel?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't remember. You see, there was four beds, and was four couples there, and a bath, you see.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the four couples, were they the same people you had traveled with from Warsaw?

speaker: interviewee
text: The same, and Julek was, and another one, friends which we met, whom we met. And the good thing is we stayed there, and that we took the statements of the customers on the other side. Because we had customers all over Poland. And Romek went traveling south to collect money, and I went traveling north to collect money.

speaker: interviewer
text: From your customers?

speaker: interviewee
text: From the customers. And they were very happy. Because you see that means, look if they had bought a tube of toothpaste or something, it was worth a fortune. And they gave us the old currency. And we collected a few bob. I was back-- in Bialystok, and Romek was not yet. And you see then it came a requisition of the hotel by the Russian army. And we went to our friends, sort of relatives of Lola. And they said they can do nothing with accommodation. But if we go 15 kilometers from there, there is a small industrial township. And they have a little house up there, and we went out to the little house.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the name of the township?

speaker: interviewee
text: Suprasl, S-U-P-R-A-S-L-I-E. And you see that [NON-ENGLISH] had two wives. My sister-in-law's brother went to [NON-ENGLISH] because she had a job in Warsaw with an agency of a cart factory. And they got immediately a job there. And we got a lot of money. And we cooked a good dinner with a lot of fat, et cetera. And we just had a fellow handyman who fixed us a stove with plenty of firewood.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember the address of this place in Bialystok?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. This is-- this was a little township.

speaker: interviewer
text: In the township.

speaker: interviewee
text: In Bialystok the place was in [NON-ENGLISH] Street but--

speaker: interviewer
text: And how long did you spend in this house?

speaker: interviewee
text: Two days, three days, four days, I don't remember. And I was a king and I had two wives who cooks, and one they come back. They went to the market and they didn't take Polish zloty. Goodness sake, I had all the Polish zloty, which I collected in the North, you see. I go to the station, to the bus station. There's no petrol. The bus is not going. So I set out by foot, 15 kilometers, took me maybe 3-4 hour walk. On the way, you see I went into a chemist's shop, who was our customer. And he lent me 3,300 rubles. And I went to the station. And I went to meet some friends who still stayed there. And they told me that Romek just boarded the bus back to Suprasl. So I boarded the next bus after him. Because at that time, at night it already was bad.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why we were going to Bialystok?

speaker: interviewee
text: Pardon?

speaker: interviewer
text: Why were you going to Bialystok?

speaker: interviewee
text: To change the money, you see. And in Bialystok you can get something. Because a lot of smugglers were going back to Poland. So they were buying the Polish money for Russian money. And I changed some to Russian money. And I went on the next bus, and we met a friend there. And said, look there's no business staying here. They're forming kibbutzes, in Vilna, which was given away by the Russians to the Lithuanians. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I come back, and Romek sits as the King, served with dinner. He said, I'm staying here. I told him, look. Look-- look, you're not staying here. Everybody you see goes to Vilna. You see, not everybody, but you see our friend who was active with the harlots. You see, where I was running the books. And we have to go. So Romek and I, we went to town to buy tickets, railway tickets to Vilna. And I stood up in the queue. And-- and every so and so minutes, they let in-- let in three people. And it looked like the queue will never end. It will take weeks. And somehow there was a fight. Romek went to have a look. How, he said, you're standing in the queue. And you keep the place for me. And he went to the door of the interest office to see how long it will take me to get the bloody tickets. And he went and got into a fight. Got into a fight and people started fighting each other, and knocking everyone over. And you see in this mix up, I just walked into the office, and I got four tickets. We went on the railway. Apparently we could have gone without tickets. Because there was no control, you see.

speaker: interviewer
text: And everything-- and to a place called Lida. Could you spell that please?

speaker: interviewee
text: L-I-D-A, and you see over there we had customers too who were buying, you see, our products. And we met there with another friend from Warsaw, who his wife was in the stationary business. And she had comments here. And we all came over to Lida. And you see, on the street there was a lot of refugees, and a lot of smugglers. And they gave us-- we didn't have any-- any accommodations. So we spent in the funeral parlor, on a-- on a-- on a wooden sort of benches. And in the morning, you see we got our place in the attic of a Jewish family, a young Jewish family. He was a painter.

words.text: on a-- on a-- on a

speaker: interviewer
text: What was his name? Do you remember?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't remember. And he had a daughter called [NON-ENGLISH]. I remember because they were forcing her to eat. But we were in the attic. There was four or five beds. And we were so tired. And so we slept over a night or so. And the next day we went to this one chemist's store and the other one, the stationary wholesaler. And they arranged smugglers to take us over the Lithuanian border. And it was all by foot. And we had to walk, or-- or go by sled, or by country wagon.

speaker: interviewer
text: What time of year was this?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was Christmas. And it took us till the night to go. You see, that means we were stopping at farms and the places we had to walk, there was a group of eight at that time, this time. And the places we had to walk, we had to go in one other's steps. You see, so that means if somebody passes through the snow, that one man was going. And then for the route, we took a whole sled and so the wind blew down the snow. And then at the next farm, we took a wagon. And the wheels were frozen, the metal was. So we ended up, you see, the three women were sick. And the five man, you see that we ended up pushing the wagon or the sled. And then we came to a little township on the border. And that was the last station. And we had to cross the border. It was New Year's eve. We're sitting in-- you see, some of the farmers were sleeping on benches. And I was frozen. My coat was frozen to the window, you know. But somehow-- you know, it was an experience. And then on the New Year, we came to a place a few steps to the border. And the locals actually could cross the borders, because they were families broken up, et cetera. And the idea was that they sent a couple of women with big bags of flax. You see, it's big and easy. And they were crossing the border. And if the guards pick them and take them to the station, the border is free. And we'll run over. And we were waiting, you see, with the watches on. And the same women came back. There's nobody on the border. Because they're celebrating the New Year on the station. Run. And all eight of us, with-- with the chief smuggler, it was a terrific organization.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was it a private run organization? Was it--

speaker: interviewee
text: No. You see the people who made-- who just made a living. And they contacted-- they contacted each other. You see, it was big money, which we paid.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what happened next?

speaker: interviewee
text: And-- and next, so we started running through the border, through the snow. And we went to-- straight to the next village across the border. We stopped at a cemetery, you see, for a rest. And the dogs were barking like bloody hell. We waited until the dogs stopped barking. And this farmer, this smuggler, took us to his brother's family.

speaker: interviewee
text: So his brother's family, we stayed there. They gave us something to eat. And-- and then all of a sudden the fellow, the owner, started yelling at his brother. You brought-- you brought the people again here. And you see that was already Lithuanian police. And we'll have trouble with the police, et cetera, et cetera.

speaker: interviewee
text: He said, look, give him a couple of watches. We quieted down. So we stayed the night over there, and started walking to Vilna. Well the same way we started to lead there, we out of there. I don't remember the name of that township. And we walked. That means we stopped at the Jewish farm, and they gave us soap. And we had magic cubes to rich bakery to the soup. And then we find out this was Jewish farm, it's Kosher. But we said nothing in the end. And somehow the next day, we came to Vilna to a certain place, you see, that's was an inn and a sort of horse station. You see the place they watered the horses, et cetera. And we were met by the [NON-ENGLISH]. They already had kibbutz. And they gave us sort of certificates that we came in August to Vilna. And when the war broke out, we can go home. And that's how we were legal in Vilna.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then we found some very sort of cheap accommodations. In Vilna?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Vilna. And we still, we had friends. Still we had customers there. And you see that means Romek and his wife went to a friend. And we got a room with one of our customers who had a chemist shop next to the, a drug store actually, next to the-- to his house. And we started sort of thinking, what to do next. Now in Vilna, we had customers. And once we met my father's customer who was buying belts and garters and braces. But I don't-- didn't have an idea of how to make a belt, you see. That means because we were going. Anyway, I picked up, at the Jewish committee, I picked up a couple of saddlers. I went to Kovno, and I bought buckles, and to shout I like to buy leather. And you see people say, and we got ourselves better accommodations after. And people said that Vilna is going to be the Switzerland of the Second World War. There's no trouble and you see, there was Russian bases in Vilna. And I started making, you see, sort of carrying belt. There was a handle. And those straps which were taken around a blanket, which you people kept the possession. That means there was a rolled up blanket, two straps, and a handle. And one of the saddlers was sewing for us, the handles. And I was just thinking it was sort of selling like hot cakes, you see.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who were you selling to?

speaker: interviewee
text: The people in the market.

speaker: interviewer
text: Not to the military?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No. Let me see. The military were bringing in families, and they were buying privately. But we had no [NON-ENGLISH]. We had no shops on the market. A fellow picked up the thing, and it was selling. And the customers of my father, you see, they took up half the production. And you see, I was going to the dentist, and I was picking up leather. And leather as an item was getting shorter and shorter. But something-- and I taught my wife to make string bags. You see, let me sew up a bag that you carry on two handles. And we bought all the string in the neighborhood. This was selling well, very well. And everything was happy. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: How would you-- how would you describe life in Vilna at that time?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, in Vilna they organized kitchens for refugees we joined you see. And for 10 cents you could get a dinner. But the women were cooking and the men were the waiters. You know? And different groups, that means we were eating at the Zionist kitchen. And there was the Intelligentsia kitchen, where you had to be an academic student. And there were the Bundist kitchen and there were the sick people kitchen. And you see, that means after the dinner, we're playing cards, we're playing chess. And we're learning English, you see. Then all of a sudden, we found the professor of English. And I formed a group, I taught them bookkeeping, and you see, that makes you young. And it is sort of an adventure. We were in touch with Warsaw all the time.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how were you in touch?

speaker: interviewee
text: Letters.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was the news you getting from Warsaw?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was very bad news, very bad news. And we sent-- you started sending them parcels. And they were receiving them.

speaker: interviewer
text: What parcels?

speaker: interviewee
text: Food. Food, because Vilna was, you see, loaded with food. You see meat and dairy products, and everything you can imagine.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember what-- what time of year this was? What the date was? Around what month of the year?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well that was January, February, March.

speaker: interviewer
text: What year?

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: '40. You see, and we were happy, you see. That means we played chess. We played checkers. There were two cafes, and we'd go and I was playing checkers with people, and I was making a few [NON-ENGLISH] out of it. Then--

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you consider trying to bring your parents over to Vilna?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. They didn't want to go. It was impossible. And you see, my sister and my brother-in-law, they were trying, you see, to do the same way. And they-- I guess stopped them at the-- at the railway station. And they came back to look after mother and father. And my father let me see, in March or April or so, I got a message that my father got himself a heart attack, and passed away. That was in the middle of that. And while we were sitting-- I can't tell you how it happened, but while we were going to Kovno, we always stepped into a cafe called Monica. And [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] we're meeting-- we're meeting all the smugglers. And somehow somebody met the Dutch consul who approached a group, and asked what language you talk, and what are you doing here. And they told him that they are refugees, and they want to go somewhere.

speaker: interviewee
text: So he said, I will give you a visa to Curacao in the Caribbean, which was a Dutch colony. And on this visa, you'll get that Japanese transit. And on the Japanese transit, you'll get a Russian transit. And then when you're out of Russia, and out of Japan, you will see something will happen. Sorry, just to clarify something. Is this-- so we'll just take a break there.
